questions.question: Explain the principle of Slotted ALOHA and compare its maximum throughput with that of pure ALOHA.
keyPoints: Slotted ALOHA divides time into slots equal to one frame duration; stations transmit only at slot boundaries.
Throughput S = G * e^{-G}, where G is the average number of attempts per slot.
Maximum throughput occurs at G = 1, yielding S_max ≈ 0.368 (36.8%).
Pure ALOHA achieves S_max ≈ 0.184 (18.4%); thus Slotted ALOHA doubles the maximum throughput.

questions.question: Explain the p‑persistent and non‑persistent CSMA protocols, and describe the Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm used in Ethernet after a collision.
keyPoints: In p‑persistent CSMA, when the channel is idle, the station transmits with probability p; otherwise it defers to the next slot with probability (1‑p).
Non‑persistent CSMA senses the channel; if idle it transmits immediately, otherwise it waits a random backoff time before sensing again.
Binary Exponential Backoff: after a collision, stations pick a random backoff counter from [0, 2^k‑1] slot times, where k = min(number of collisions, 10); each slot equals 512 bit times.
If 16 successive collisions occur, the frame is discarded.

questions.question: Describe the token passing operation in Token Ring and Token Bus networks. Include the stages of token acquisition, frame transmission, token recovery, and priority handling.
keyPoints: A token is a special control frame that circulates; only the station holding the token may transmit.
In Token Ring, the token moves around the ring; after transmitting, the station releases a new token (or retains it if more frames are queued).
Token Bus uses a logical token passed on a linear bus according to a predefined order, with token‑holding timers to limit transmission duration.
Token loss recovery employs beaconing or token‑reclaim procedures, and priority bits in the token allow higher‑priority traffic to preempt.

questions.question: Explain the CSMA/CD protocol used in Ethernet, covering collision detection, jam signal, minimum frame size, and the binary exponential backoff algorithm. Then compare the IEEE 802.11 CSMA/CA MAC with the earlier MACA protocol used for wireless LANs. Finally, briefly describe the three channelization techniques—TDMA, FDMA, and CDMA—used in cellular and satellite systems.
keyPoints: CSMA/CD: stations listen before transmitting; collisions are detected within 2τ (round‑trip propagation time), after which a jam signal is sent to all stations.

The minimum Ethernet frame size (64 bytes) ensures that collisions are detected before transmission ends; after a collision, Binary Exponential Backoff doubles the backoff window up to 1023 slot times.
IEEE 802.11 DCF uses CSMA/CA with ACK, RTS/CTS, and random backoff; MACA (Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) avoids hidden nodes via RTS/CTS but lacks ACK and is less robust.

TDMA allocates distinct time slots, FDMA divides the bandwidth into separate frequency bands, and CDMA uses orthogonal spreading codes; each supports simultaneous users with differing capacity, complexity, and interference characteristics.
